params.text:
Working relationships and team working

In care you build many relationships — with the people you support, with their families, and with colleagues and other professionals such as nurses, GPs and social workers. It is important to understand the difference between a working relationship and a personal relationship.

  • A working relationship is professional. It is based on your role, guided by policies and boundaries, and focused on the person’s needs and agreed outcomes.
  • A personal relationship is one you choose — with friends and family — based on emotional ties and personal history, with no professional boundaries or accountability.

Keeping professional boundaries protects the people you support and keeps care fair, safe and consistent. Team working and partnership working matter too: sharing information appropriately, respecting each other’s roles, and working together all lead to better, safer care.

params.question: The relationship between a care worker and a person they support is a personal friendship rather than a professional working relationship.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. Although it can be warm and caring, it is a professional working relationship with clear boundaries, not a personal friendship.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. It is a professional working relationship — guided by your role, boundaries and policies — not a personal friendship.
